late nineteenth century. By the early twentieth century, German law had incorporated a

wide variety of “personality” rights into its statutory schemes. The scandal over

Bismarck’s deathbed photos led, in 1907, to the introduction of statutory protections for

one’s image, as part of a larger scheme regulating rights in works of art. 135 Meanwhile

the German Civil Code, which went into force in 1900, included protections against the

appropriation of one’s name, 136 and the impairment of one’s credit, 137 alongside

protections for life, body, health and liberty. 138 The law on unfair competition of 1909

132
    For Gareis, see Dieter Leuze, Die Entwicklung des Persönlichkeitsrechts im 19. Jahrhundert (Bielefeld:
Gieseking, 1962), 93-103.
133
    See Kohler, Das Autorrecht: Eine zivilistische Abhandlung (Jena: Fischer, 1880), 123-159, and 126 for
use of “Persönlichkeit.” On Kohler, see further Leuze, Entwicklung des Personlichkeitsrechts, 103-111. In
his mature reflections, Kohler in effect rejected both Roman and Germanic approaches in ways that deserve
more discussion than I can give them here. See e.g. his Das Eigenbild im Recht (Berlin: Guttentag, 1903).
134
    The famous Bismarck case was preceded by a couple of other photography cases, involving a woman in
a bathing suit and a photograph used to advertise a hair dye. It was the Bismarck matter that really caught
the public imagination in Germany. See Osterrieth, Urheberrecht, 161, describing the origns of the
protections for one’s image contained in §§ 22- 24 KUG. [NB: O. also mentions protections for portraits
and portrait-busts —but just for Besteller, not for Abgebildete.]
135
    Not protecting the image, the official government draft for this law explained, would not do justice to
“the respect which is owed to the personality.” Regierungsvorlage to §§ 22-24 KUG, reproduced in
Osterrieth, Urheberrecht, 161. The image needed to be protected in a way that would “leave freedom of
movement for the respectable press, without leaving justifiable private interests without protection.” Id.,
cited in Osterrieth,Urheberrecht, 163.
136
    §12 BGB.
137
    §824 BGB.
138
    §823 BGB.

46
 included a characteristically German provision protecting enterprises against untrue

statements that harmed their operations or their credit, 139 and the Bismarck-era law on

freedom of the press granted a right to respond. 140 Perhaps most importantly, the

Criminal Code included its prohibition on insults. 141 All of this added up to protections

that were hardly insignificant by the eve of the First World War. The Weimar era saw a

number of further important cases, of which the most famous involved (once again)

royalty. This was a successful suit brought by ex-Kaiser Wilhelm over a stage play

portraying him. 142

In short, there were plenty of “personality” protections in German law by the

early part of the twentieth century. Nevertheless the Civil Code itself, which went into
